questions.question: The highest airport in the United States is Leadville-Lake County at 9,934 feet. On a warm 86°F day, what is your density altitude with a pressure of 30.25" Hg?
You must perform your commercial pilot steep turns at 50° of bank. What load factor (Gs) does your aircraft experience in a 50° bank turn at 115 KTAS?
You are flying at M0.83 at FL390 and ISA+7°C. What is your indicated airspeed?

A certain cartoon character resides in a pineapple under the sea. For every 33 feet of depth, the water pressure increases by 1 standard atmosphere. If Spongebob feels 8258 millibars of pressure, and the airport at sea level is reporting 30.22" Hg, how deep is Spongebob's house?
What is the density altitude at an indicated altitude of 2,500 feet, pressure of 30.30" Hg, OAT of -15°F?

You decide to relax and take your decked-out Piper Seminole for a ride. While cruising at 8,000 feet on the dot, thanks to the ol' wing leveler, at a blistering -40°C and altimeter setting of 29.52" Hg, what is your current true altitude?
Reference the previous question. Now imagine strapping a rocket to your Piper Seminole. If you now are cookin' at 680 knots TAS, what Mach number are you flying at?
questions.context: Referenced question 32: You decide to relax and take your decked-out Piper Seminole for a ride. While cruising at 8,000 feet on the dot, thanks to the ol' wing leveler, at a blistering -40°C and altimeter setting of 29.52" Hg, what is your current true altitude?

questions.question: A commercial Steep Turn requires 50° of bank for the maneuver. What is the load factor for this bank?
What is the density altitude at 5,500' indicated, with pressure at 1010 Mb and temperature of -15°F?
You are cruising at 6,500' MSL, the altimeter setting is 29.56"Hg, and the temperature is ISA -12°C. If your indicated airspeed is 129 mph, what is your Mach number?
What is the density altitude on a hot summer day (97°F) at a mountainous airport (Elev. 4911') with an altimeter setting of 29.65" Hg?
What is the speed of sound at 10,000' MSL on a standard day?
You are about to depart your home airport 608' MSL. Local altimeter setting is 29.64" Hg and the temperature is 84°F. What is the density altitude?
If bank angle is increased from 45° to 60°, by what percentage would load factor increase?
What is the density altitude at 6,500 feet indicated if the pressure is 993 mb and the temperature is -34°F?
What is the true airspeed equivalent of Mach 0.75 at FL410 with OAT of -56°C?

You need to replace the tie-down ropes for your parking "T" on the ramp. You need three separate ropes, one for each wing and one for the tail. The wing tie-downs are 1.68 m above the ground. The tail tie-down is 1.07 m above the asphalt. If you add 24 inches for tying knots, how much total rope do you need to buy?

According to your performance calculations, you'll need 716 meters of runway to clear the standard 50' obstacle at the end of the runway. However, the winds reported by the tower are 340° at 16 kts. You're departing from runway 2. The charts allow you to subtract 50' for every 2 kts of headwind or add 100' for every knot of tailwind. What is your corrected takeoff distance?

Your anti-skid system monitors wheel speed during landing and releases the brake if the wheel speed is below 80% of the aircraft ground speed. Your aircraft has 16" diameter tires, and the normal touchdown speed is 97 knots. Below what wheel speed will the anti-skid trigger when the aircraft is at touchdown speed?
A 48" x 40" pallet is loaded with a Packard V-1650 Merlin engine weighing in at 746 kg. How many pounds per square inch of pressure is being exerted on the pallet?

Pivotal altitude is calculated by dividing 11.3 from the squared ground speed in knots and adding field elevation to the quotient. The ground speed is 200 mph and the field elevation is 1029', what is the pivotal altitude?

If you are cruising at FL280 and 225 knots calibrated airspeed, by how many degrees would the indicated temperature be higher than actual outside air temperature if the true airspeed is 336 knots? Assume your air data computer uses a recovery coefficient of 1.0.
Your landing ground roll increases 10% for every 4 kt increase in airspeed at touchdown. If you normally require 560' when touching down at 36 kts, how much runway will you use if you land at 44 kts?

You decide to do a groundspeed check while tracking your progress with a nearby VOR. It takes you 4:54 to fly between the 005° and 350° radials. If your DME reads 35 nm, what is your actual groundspeed?
Your friend asks you to show him what 1.8g feels like. You don't have a g-meter in your aircraft, so as you roll into a level turn, what bank angle do you need to maintain?

Your performance chart indicates a takeoff roll of 930' with the following notes: [[NEXT_LINE]] - Increase takeoff roll by 7% of charted distance for every 10°C above ISA. [[NEXT_LINE]] - Increase takeoff roll by 4% of charted distance for every 1% runway upslope. [[NEXT_LINE]] - Decrease takeoff roll by 9% of charted distance for every 10 knots of headwind. [[NEXT_LINE]] If you depart from runway 13 at sea level, the OAT is 28°C, the runway has a 1.6% upslope, and the wind is 180° at 13 knots, what is your takeoff ground roll?
You are practicing turns around a point, and while flying at 115 KTAS, you notice that it takes 46 seconds to complete a 360° turn. How far are you from the point?

You are flying at FL320 and trying to navigate around thunderstorms. Your weather radar is tilted -2.5° and the bottom of the radar beam is painting the ground (4,000' MSL) as displayed on the 40 nm ring on the MFD. Your radar has a 7-degree beam width. You want to place the center of the radar beam at the freezing level (FL120) at 40 nm to get the best picture of reflectivity. What tilt angle should you set the radar?

Airflow is the primary means of cooling an engine. The manufacturer has determined that decelerating from 100 KTS to 95 KTS, the engine is warmed by 7°F. However, for every 5-knot reduction in airspeed, the rate of temperature increase is reduced by 2.5%. Cruising at 90 knots the engine indicates 170°F. After slowing to 47 KTS, what is the new engine temperature?
Reference the previous question. With a glide ratio of 9:1 at what altitude would you have to be to glide to shore from the middle of the lake in calm wind? Lake elevation is 650'.
questions.context: Referenced question 17: You are flying to Oshkosh and debating flying over Lake Michigan, or going around. The narrowest portion is 56 NM along a 286° course cruising at 106 KTAS. If the winds are 325° at 15 kts how far after leaving the eastern shore would it be an equal amount of time to continue or turn around?

questions.question: Just before turning on to runway 15 (true heading 141°), you calculate your takeoff distance. The POH shows 981 feet for a dry runway with a note to increase 7% for every 4° Celsius above standard and decrease 4% for every 5° Celsius below standard. It then notes to increase the distance by 10% when wet. If the elevation is 7520' and the temperature is 59°F, how many meters will you travel before you lift off?
The Datum is 495 cm forward of the nose of the aircraft. How far from the nose is your CG?

The POH lists takeoff distance at sea level of 1,200 feet. It also says to increase takeoff distance 9% for each 1,000' above a standard sea level day. Referencing the previous question, what is the corrected takeoff distance?
questions.context: Referenced question 29: It's a hot 95°F day as you depart from an airport elevation of 3,750'. If the current altimeter is 30.17" Hg, what is the density altitude?

questions.question: A news helicopter is hovering when it sees an airliner flying across its field of view from an 11 o'clock position to a 1 o'clock position in 61 seconds at the same altitude. If the aircraft are 4 SM apart, what is the airliner's airspeed?
Each wing of your 1959 Cessna 182 is 16 feet long and 3 feet wide. If you take off at 2600 LBS and make an immediate 50° bank turn, what is the wing loading in the turn?

After performing a rejected takeoff at 98 knots, you want to know how hot your brakes are. Looking at the manufacturer testing data, you see that maximum braking at 120 knots will increase brake temperatures by 35°C per 10 knots of speed reduction. Additionally, they note that for every 10 knots of speed reduction, the rate of temperature increase is reduced by 6%. If you applied maximum braking until reaching 20 knots, what were your brake temperatures?
From the previous question, If your friend departs at the same time as you do, what TAS would he have to fly to land at the same time as you?
questions.context: Referenced question 14: You and your friend decide to go to Las Vegas for the weekend. You depart from Mesquite (70 NM away) on a course of 219° and 105 KTAS. If your friend wants to arrive at the same time as you (1548Z), and he is departing from St. George on a course of 223° and 98 NM, what time would he have to depart if he wants to cruise at 105 KTAS? (Wind is 265° at 23 KTS)

questions.question: At the airport you are flying over (elev. 1,265') the AWOS is reporting a temperature of 10°C and altimeter setting of 29.67" Hg. At your cruise altitude of 7,500' MSL the temperature is -10°C. What is your true altitude?
How tall is a redwood tree if you are lying on the ground gazing at it from 1/4 SM away? The angle from your eyes to the top of the tree is 11.76°.
Cruising over Mount Adams (12,120') at 14,500', if your altimeter is set to 30.32" Hg and the OAT is -23°F what is your true altitude?

The max takeoff weight of a B737 (two-engine passenger jet) is 85,139 LBS and each engine produces 13,150 LBS of thrust. While the Challenger 300's (two-engine corporate jet) max takeoff weight is 38,850 LBS and each engine produces 6,826 LBS of thrust. How much better is the Challenger 300's thrust to weight ratio?

With the applicable FAA approvals taken care of, you are using your camera drone to take a video of your friend's takeoff in her newly restored Globe Swift. The Swift has a takeoff roll of 970', and it takes 11 seconds to reach liftoff speed. Your drone has a maximum speed of 35 MPH. You will fly the drone parallel to the runway at maximum speed to capture the takeoff. If you want the Swift to lift off exactly when it is abeam the drone, how long should your friend wait after the drone passes to begin her takeoff roll?

At the airport you are flying over (elevation 1,265') where the AWOS is reporting a temperature of 10°C and altimeter of 29.67" Hg. At your cruise altitude of 7,500' MSL the temperature is -10°C. What is your true altitude?

You are cruising at an indicated altitude of 10,500 feet MSL. You notice you are passing over Dixon, WY (elev. 6,549) and tune in the AWOS to get the latest weather. It is reporting a temperature of 32°C and an altimeter setting of 29.77" Hg. Assuming a standard temperature lapse rate, what is your true altitude?
What is the square root of 302?

While flying in Alaska, you are concerned about terrain clearance because of the extremely cold temperatures. If you are flying at 8,500', the altimeter setting is 30.47, and the OAT is -25°C, how far below indicated altitude are you actually flying?

As you are flying, you notice a large wildfire in front of you. After reporting the fire to ATC, you turn 60° to the right to get around the smoke. Once you are abeam the fire, you turn left to intercept your original course at a 60° angle. If you rejoin your course 9 NM past where you started deviating, how much distance did you travel during the deviation?

The manufacturer testing data notes that your airplane experiences 450 lbs. of parasite drag at a normal cruise speed of 145 knots. You remember from your aerodynamics class that parasite drag increases with the square of an increase in airspeed. If you slow down to 60 knots, how much parasite drag is the airplane experiencing?

As you break out of the clouds, you see the MALSR lighting system at the approach end of the runway. You remember from instrument ground school that the RAIL (runway alignment indicator light, i.e. "rabbit") is 800' long and the lights flash in sequence twice per second. How fast does the "rabbit" appear to move?

You are approaching your home airport on a 346 course. When you are 18 nm away, you hear your friend call up on the CTAF, and she reports her position as 13 nm and flying a 256 course. How far away from your friend are you?

Your aircraft has two altimeters. On the ground, Altimeter A reads 5,360 feet, and Altimeter B reads 5,420 feet. If the difference between the altimeters increases by 10% for every 10,000 feet you climb, what will Altimeter B read if Altimeter A indicates 35,000 feet?

Your normal takeoff distance is 1,270' with 430' of that occurring after liftoff. If your aircraft operating handbook says to increase your normal takeoff by 40% of the ground roll when operating on grass, what would be your normal takeoff distance when departing a grass runway?
You are climbing at a constant rate, at 15° nose up. If each of your two engines produces 8700 lbs. of thrust, what is the vertical component of thrust?

You calculate a short-field ground roll distance of 593'. You will depart runway 30. The current winds are 280° at 14 KTS. Your POH instructs you to subtract 12% from the ground roll for every 15 KTS of headwind. What will your new calculated short-field ground roll distance be?
How many gallons of engine oil does it take to balance a see-saw with 40 gallons of AVGAS on the other end?

Stanley departs KABC at 8:45 AM and instantly accelerates to his cruise speed of 226 KTS. He flies directly towards KXYZ. Gordon departs KXYZ at 9:05 AM and immediately accelerates to his cruise speed of 170 MPH. He flies directly toward KABC. If the two airports are 420 NM apart, at what time will Stanley and Gordon pass each other? Both airports are located within the same time zone.

In the last question, we assumed that the fuel leak rate had been constant since takeoff. Unfortunately, this was not the case. On the way to the operationally favorable diversion airport at 2300' AGL, your only engine has stopped producing power. Your fuel flow gauge shows zero GALS per hour. Your fuel quantity gauge shows zero on both sides. How did you not catch this sooner? There is an airport 5.4 NM away and the average glide angle to the airport will yield 400'/NM. Do you have sufficient energy to make it to that airport? Choose the answer that best describes your situation.

choices.text: No, you will be .3 NM short.
No, your altitude at the airport would have been -320' AGL (BGL?).
Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 140' AGL.
Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 340' AGL.

questions.correctChoice: Yes, your altitude at the airport will be 140' AGL.
questions.context: Referenced question 16: Imagine that the fuel quantity gauges are showing a total of 50 GALS on board, and the "total fuel used" gauge is showing 27.2 GALS used over the past 2 HRS and 17 MINS. At departure, you had 86 GALS on board. You have a fuel leak between the tank and the fuel flow sensor that is causing the difference, and you want to know if you'll have enough fuel to get to the more operationally favorable diversion airport, rather than the closest in point of time. To get to the operationally favorable diversion airport you will burn 13 GALS cruise, descent, and landing, and those steps will require 48 MINS. You'd like to land with at least 6 GALS on board as a safe reserve. If you assume that the leak rate has been constant since takeoff, and divert now, how much fuel will you have on landing at the operationally favorable diversion airport?

questions.question: You have been hired to tow a banner over Springfield's 81st annual Cherry Blossom Festival. Your instructions are to fly a perfect circle around the fairgrounds at 1500' AGL, exactly 4.3 NM from the center of the action. After a long shift (5 HRS and 22 MINS), you have flown 18 laps around the festivities. The weather is beautiful, with a temperature of ISA + 4, and an altimeter setting of 30.17". The elevation in the area is 611' MSL. What was your average groundspeed?

You and your 6 friends go out for dinner and decide to split the check equally. If the bill subtotal came to $238, the tax rate is 5.75%, and you want to tip 18% of the total bill, how much do you add to your own bill for the tip?
Find the true altitude when flying at 4,317' on a sunny 94°F day. The altimeter setting is 29.55" Hg.

Barb is practicing ground reference maneuvers today. She sees rows of trees spaced a statute mile apart perpendicular to a highway. She picks the intersection of a row of trees and the highway and sets up for a turn around that point. She maintains a constant radius in the turn and starts when she is over the highway and halfway between two rows of trees. If her average speed in the turn is 85 MPH, how long does it take to complete one full circle?

What is the temperature at 12,000' if you notice that you cross 2 section lines in 33 seconds with a true course of 053° at a CAS of 170 mph? Your altimeter setting is 29.67" Hg and your compass shows a heading of 060°. Variation is 5E and your compass deviation is -5. Winds aloft are 171°@57 knots.

The POH for your single engine aircraft specifies a 12:1 glide ratio if the engine were to fail. If you were cruising at 8,000 feet over the Gulf of Mexico, how far away from the shoreline could you be if you wanted to remain within gliding range of land?

You need to build multi engine time to apply for your first airline job, so you rent a twin at the local FBO with some friends. After the airplane is loaded, you find you have a weight of 4,329 pounds and a moment of 203,463 IN-LBS. What is your CG?
A German sailplane with a 28 meter wingspan has a glide ratio of 65:1. How far will it glide in a straight line from 7,500' AGL to touchdown under calm winds?

Fresh from a long range navigation lecture where you learned about finding a course using latitude and longitude coordinates, you wish to impress your CFI at your upcoming lesson. You tell him that the distance between a degree of latitude is 60 NM and the distance between a degree of longitude can be calculated since it varies from 60 NM at the equator to zero at the Earth's poles. So he says, "Ok, hot shot, using these points, one at N48°30' W101°22' and the other at N48°30' W99°47', what is the distance between them?"
If the previous takeoff roll distance is to be reduced by 28 feet for each knot of headwind component, the winds are 330@15 mph, and the takeoff runway is 26, what is the wind-corrected takeoff roll?
questions.context: Referenced question 1: If the takeoff roll distance of an airplane is 283 yards, and the distance to clear a 50 foot obstacle is 145% of the takeoff roll, what is the total takeoff distance required to clear the obstacle?

questions.question: Reading your altitude of 500' (with 30.01" altimeter setting) on final you realize you forgot to set the current local altimeter setting, 30.24". What is your actual altitude AGL if the elevation is 133'?
You are doing a turn around a point and you are maintaining 90 knots and in one full circle you travel 2.6 nm. How far from the point are you maintaining on average?

You are transporting a drum with 48 gallons of AVGAS to northern Alaska. The drum sits on a pallet measuring 24 inches square. Your aircraft has a floor load limit of 80 lbs/square feet. Can you load the barrel with the pallet it rests on now and remain within the limitation? (disregard the weight of the drum and pallet itself)

choices.text: No, the pallet needs to be 0.6 square feet larger
Yes, the pallet is large enough by 0.4 square feet
Yes, the pallet is large enough by 0.8 square feet
No, the pallet needs to be 8 square feet larger

questions.correctChoice: Yes, the pallet is large enough by 0.4 square feet

questions.question: Your engine has been acting funny during climb, but it is a beautiful day so you decide to go fly anyway. After takeoff you climb at 830 fpm for a mere 1.7 minutes before your engine fails. With a 14:1 glide ratio, how far will you travel before crash landing.
Your instructor asks you if you can circle a point with a 3/4 statute mile radius at a standard rate (3° per second). What TAS must you maintain in order to achieve this?

At 165 knots in cruise on a 173° course, ATC advises that you have converging traffic 500 feet above you that is 60° off your nose to the left and 7 NM away. Their course is 233° and they are flying at 210 knots. Assuming calm winds, how long will it take until you cross the flight path of that traffic and will they be to the left, to the right, or directly above you?

choices.text: 0:02:33; the traffic is to your left
0:09:20; the traffic is directly above you
0:01:55; the traffic is to your left
0:02:33; the traffic is to your right

questions.correctChoice: 0:02:33; the traffic is to your right

questions.question: Traveling at 145 mph, you are instructed to do a left 360° turn. If you decide to do a standard 3° per sec turn how many miles do you travel in the turn?
Using the above information what was the diameter of your turn?
questions.context: Referenced question 21: Traveling at 145 mph, you are instructed to do a left 360° turn. If you decide to do a standard 3° per sec turn how many miles do you travel in the turn?

questions.question: If your standard ground roll is 870' and the POH reads to add 25% for a grass runway then 12% of the computed value for every 1% of upslope, then subtract 9% of the computed value for every 12 KTS of headwind, what would your actual ground roll be on runway 35 (which is grass) that has a .6% upslope with the wind 040° at 12 KTS?
Using the above information, how much headwind component do you need to have a ground roll of 870' on a level grass runway?
questions.context: Referenced question 26: If your standard ground roll is 870' and the POH reads to add 25% for a grass runway then 12% of the computed value for every 1% of upslope, then subtract 9% of the computed value for every 12 KTS of headwind, what would your actual ground roll be on runway 35 (which is grass) that has a .6% upslope with the wind 040° at 12 KTS?

questions.question: While descending through 18,000 feet into Atlanta-Hartsfield (elev. 1026 ft. MSL), you forget to set your altimeter to the local altimeter setting. If your altimeter is still set to the standard altimeter setting and barometric pressure is 30.24 in. Hg, what will your altimeter read when you pull into the gate?

If the fighter's average wind is 010@52 miles per hour and course to the intercept point is 046°, using the information from the previous question and an average cost of $13,500 per hour, what is the taxpayer expense for him to get to the intercept point?
questions.context: In the preflight briefing for your upcoming 328 statute mile cross country trip you learn that there will be a TFR directly under your true course of 332 degrees. The center of the TFR will be located 185 nautical miles along your route. The TFR is 5 nautical miles across, from the surface to 7,000 feet, and will be active from 1400Z to 1600Z. Use this information to answer the next 7 questions. [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 15: The fighter has an average speed of 582 knots and has to travel 304 nautical miles to his intercept point. He'll climb at 5,800 feet per minute from sea level to 17,000 feet. How long will that climb take?

questions.question: How many miles did the aircraft fly in cruise?
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 20 through 23. [[NEXT_LINE]] Wind (true): 285° at 27 knots at cruise, calm winds for climb and descent [[NEXT_LINE]] Standard temperature and pressure [[NEXT_LINE]] Variation: 7W [[NEXT_LINE]] Magnetic heading: 159° [[NEXT_LINE]] Total distance: 206 statute miles [[NEXT_LINE]] Total fuel burn: 14.48 US gallons [[NEXT_LINE]] Departure elevation: 1,350' [[NEXT_LINE]] Destination elevation: 820' [[NEXT_LINE]] Climb: 10m15s and 2.27 US gallons of fuel, 84 knot groundspeed [[NEXT_LINE]] Cruise: 127.5 knot groundspeed [[NEXT_LINE]] Descent: 8m21s and 0.86 US gallons of fuel, 800 fpm descent rate, 122 knot groundspeed

questions.question: What was the climb rate and descent fuel burn rate?
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 20 through 23. [[NEXT_LINE]] Wind (true): 285° at 27 knots at cruise, calm winds for climb and descent [[NEXT_LINE]] Standard temperature and pressure [[NEXT_LINE]] Variation: 7W [[NEXT_LINE]] Magnetic heading: 159° [[NEXT_LINE]] Total distance: 206 statute miles [[NEXT_LINE]] Total fuel burn: 14.48 US gallons [[NEXT_LINE]] Departure elevation: 1,350' [[NEXT_LINE]] Destination elevation: 820' [[NEXT_LINE]] Climb: 10m15s and 2.27 US gallons of fuel, 84 knot groundspeed [[NEXT_LINE]] Cruise: 127.5 knot groundspeed [[NEXT_LINE]] Descent: 8m21s and 0.86 US gallons of fuel, 800 fpm descent rate, 122 knot groundspeed

questions.question: What is your ramp CG?
questions.context: Use the following loading information to answer questions 25 through 29: [[TABLE]]

questions.question: Your old Cub is reaching TBO and burns oil at 6 oz./hour. If the total capacity of the oil reservoir is 5.5 quarts, how much oil do you need to add after adding 4.5 hours of flight time to the logbook?

At maximum takeoff weight, the B757 takes 7000 feet to rotate at standard temperature. Its roll increases 10% for every 10° above standard. DFW is 625 feet MSL. Assuming the runway is 13,401 feet long, and the plane rotates with 5000 feet of runway left, what was the temperature at DFW? No wind.
Given the following information, find true altitude MSL. Elevation = 6,531' MSL; Indicated altitude = 12,500' MSL; Temperature = 22°C; Altimeter setting = 29.33" Hg; Indicated airspeed = 188 knots.
Find true altitude AGL. Elevation 3720'. Cruising at 9500' MSL. Altimeter 30.21" Hg. Temperature is -23°C.
Find pressure altitude. Elevation 7770'. Cruising at 17,500' MSL, -22°C, Altimeter 29.29" Hg.

You decide to live on the edge and fly a Cessna 172 from Palm Beach International, Florida to Grand Bahama International, Bahamas. Using the following information, how far from Grand Bahama will you be when it will take just as long to continue to Grand Bahama as it will to turn around and go back to Palm Beach? [[NEXT_LINE]] Distance between airports: 76 nm [[NEXT_LINE]] Pressure Altitude: 7,000' MSL [[NEXT_LINE]] Indicated Airspeed: 116 KIAS [[NEXT_LINE]] OAT: 6°C [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course from Palm Beach: 095° [[NEXT_LINE]] True Wind: 302° at 39 mph [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel On Board/Fuel Flow: 48 US gallons / 63 lb./hr.

An aircraft is loaded as such: Empty Weight 2300 lbs at 75.0" 2 Forward Passengers (150 lbs each) 300 lbs at 70.0" 2 Aft Passengers (170 lbs each) 340 lbs at 95.0" 40 US Gallons of AVGAS 240 lbs at 80.0" 50 lbs of cargo 50 lbs at 125.0" What is the weight and balance of this loaded aircraft?

An aircraft (Empty Weight and CG of 1950 lbs at 65.0") has a CG limit between 67.5" and 73.0" and a maximum takeoff weight of 2850 lbs. The pilot (170 lbs), Tom (180 lbs), Samantha (120 lbs), and Laura (110 lbs) are flying to Orlando with 50 US Gallons of AVGAS (at 80.0"). The pilot and Samantha are up front (at 55.0") and Tom and Laura are in back (at 90.0"). Can all the passengers go to Orlando within legal limits?

choices.text: Yes, by only 20 lbs and just barely inside the CG limit
Yes, by 120 lbs and well with CG limit
No, outside of the CG envelope

questions.correctChoice: Yes, by only 20 lbs and just barely inside the CG limit

questions.question: At a pressure altitude of 24,000 feet with an OAT of -14°C and a calibrated altitude of 24,000 feet, what is the true altitude?
At takeoff from an airport (8820 ft MSL), with an altimeter of 29.63", and a temperature of 91°F, what is the true altitude?
Flying at an indicated altitude of 9500 feet, the OAT is -12°C with an altimeter setting of 30.13". What is the true altitude?
What is the weight of the Rear Passengers?
questions.context: questions 3 through 6. [[DIVIDER]] Given: [[NEXT_LINE]] Weight Station Mom/1000 Fill in the above table to answer

questions.question: What is the Moment for the Front Passengers?
questions.context: questions 3 through 6. [[DIVIDER]] Given: [[NEXT_LINE]] Weight Station Mom/1000 Fill in the above table to answer

questions.question: What is the Station for the Bags?
questions.context: questions 3 through 6. [[DIVIDER]] Given: [[NEXT_LINE]] Weight Station Mom/1000 Fill in the above table to answer

questions.question: What is the Moment for the Fuel?
questions.context: questions 3 through 6. [[DIVIDER]] Given: [[NEXT_LINE]] Weight Station Mom/1000 Fill in the above table to answer

questions.question: What is the standard temperature at 19,500'?
What is the True Altitude if at your Indicated Altitude of 7500' the temperature is 48°F and the altimeter setting 28.97"?
The miles of flight during cruise will be
questions.context: Use the following to answer questions 27 - 31. You're departing Hometown airport (elevation 2280') with a 400 fpm rate of climb to a cruise altitude of 6500 feet. Your groundspeed in the climb is 85 kts and in cruise it is 120 kts. You descend to 1000 feet above destination airport (elevation 1700) with a groundspeed of 140 kts and a de scent rate of 650 fpm. Your fuel consumption is 11 gph in climb, 9.5 gph in cruise and 7 gph in descent. You are carrying 27 gallons of usable fuel. The total distance of the flight is 295 nm.

questions.question: What is your true altitude? Average ground speed: 113 kts. [[NEXT_LINE]] True course: 280°. [[NEXT_LINE]] Wind: 120@12 kts. [[NEXT_LINE]] Indicated altitude: 10.000' [[NEXT_LINE]] Outside air temperature: -10°F Pressure altitude same as indicated
What is your true altitude? Given: [[NEXT_LINE]] Pressure altitude: 12,500' [[NEXT_LINE]] Calibrated altitude: 12,500' [[NEXT_LINE]] Outside air temperature: -12°C

While preparing to take a load of cargo to a remote backcountry airstrip in the desert, you observe two steel 55-gallon drums. The freight forwarder has told you that one is completely filled with oil and that one is completely filled with AVGAS, but he can't remember which one is which. The following questions are based on this situation: Your destination has ordered fuel only. In an attempt to determine which drum should go, you decide to use a scale. The first drum that you try weighs 355 pounds, so you decide:

choices.text: that your manual flight computer has dead batteries
to take the first drum that you weighed, since you are believe that it is fuel
that the first barrel could not possibly be fuel
that the scale has an error of 85 pounds

questions.correctChoice: to take the first drum that you weighed, since you are believe that it is fuel

questions.question: The floor in your cargo area is only approved for a load of 50 pounds per square foot. If you put the drum on a 25- pound pallet that is 36" square, will you even be able to take a 55-gallon drum of fuel?

choices.text: Yes, and there is a 5% safety margin.
Yes, and you have 8 pounds per square foot to spare.
No, you cannot take the fuel because the floor load is exceeded
No, and the floor load limit is exceeded by 16 pounds per square foot.

questions.correctChoice: Yes, and you have 8 pounds per square foot to spare.

questions.question: Perhaps the pilot of the first aircraft has a rusty instrument scan, and actually flies 10 mph slower than his assigned speed. If the wind picks up to a 5-knot headwind component, what will his ground speed be on final?
If the OAT is 20°C, the Pressure Altitude is 8000 feet, and the Calibrated Altitude is 8500 feet, what is the True Altitude?
If the airport elevation is 5805 feet and the altimeter setting is 30.57 inches of mercury, what is the pressure altitude?
You are flying at an Indicated Altitude of 6,000 feet; OAT is -27°C; and the altimeter setting is 29.78" Hg. What is the pressure altitude and true altitude?
What is your true altitude?
You are flying at an Indicated Altitude of 12,500 feet; OAT is -16°C and the altimeter setting is 30.41" Hg. What is the true altitude?

What is your true altitude? Given: [[NEXT_LINE]] Pressure altitude:10,500' [[NEXT_LINE]] Calibrated altitude:11,500' [[NEXT_LINE]] Outside air temperate: -12 C _____________________________________ (Whole number) Write your answer on the answer sheet

A twin engine aircraft is in cruise and will pass over checkpoint A at 1605Z. Its route of flight will then take it directly to checkpoint B, 83 nautical miles away, with a groundspeed of 162 mph. If a single engine aircraft traveling the exact opposite flight path at 90 kts groundspeed flies over checkpoint B at 1630Z, at what time will the two aircraft cross paths?
How many nautical miles will you spend in cruise? (Write your answer on the answer sheet to the nearest mile.)
What is the square root of 178?

One afternoon you are preparing for a lesson in a Cessna 172, and your instructor provides you with this hypothetical situation: At 7500 ft MSL, you lose your engine. The winds aloft are 120° at 25 knots all the way to the surface. Airport #1 (field elevation of 3100 ft MSL) is 10 NM on a true course of 310°. Airport #2 (field elevation of 350 ft MSL) is 13 NM on a true course of 145°. Your glide rate of descent is 700 FPM and your average TAS is 80 knots. Assuming instantaneous turns to the airports. [[NEXT_LINE]] Which, if any, airport can make it to?

choices.text: You can make both but only barely by 0.1 to 0.25 nautical miles.
You come up short to both airports by more than 1 nautical mile.
You come up short to Airport #1 by 1.2 nautical miles but can land at Airport #2.
You can land at Airport #1 but come up short to Airport #2 by 3.2 nautical miles.

questions.correctChoice: You can land at Airport #1 but come up short to Airport #2 by 3.2 nautical miles.

questions.question: The altimeter setting in your area is 30.87" Hg. Your current indicated altitude is 7500 ft with an OAT of -20°C. You travel a couple of hundred miles to another area where the new altimeter setting is 29.67" Hg. and an OAT of +20°C. How much does your true altitude change when traveling between the first area to the second area?

You takeoff using the calculations from question 27. Just prior to the halfway point the CEO comes up to the flight deck and asks, "What if we changed altitudes at the halfway point to 30,000 feet from 36,000 feet? Can we get there faster?" If the change is instantaneous, you say:

choices.text: Yes, we would get there 3 minutes faster.
Yes, we would get there 8 minutes faster.
Yes, we would get there 13 minutes faster.
No, we would be there 3 minutes later

questions.correctChoice: Yes, we would get there 13 minutes faster.
questions.context: NIKE is interviewing you for a position flying their Gulfstream V. They are asking you some hypothetical questions to gage your response. Some background information is that you have 13,000 lbs of fuel onboard. The interviewer says for this flight the GV burns fuel at 2000 lbs/hr (above 32,000 feet) and 2200 lbs/hr (below 32,000 feet). Flying at 0.78M with the following winds aloft: [[TABLE]] You are traveling 2120 miles from Hillsboro, OR (HIO) to Honolulu, HI (HNL). The True Course from HIO to HNL is 232°. [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 27: Using the above information, your next selection is to look at 36,000 feet. What is the time en route and fuel burn, for the entire flight?

questions.question: Two aircraft fly over the same VOR at the same time at different altitudes. Aircraft Alpha flies outbound on the 283° radial and aircraft Bravo flies outbound on the 037° radial. At the time aircraft Alpha is reading 40 DME, aircraft Bravo is reading 35 DME. At this point, how far apart are the aircraft from each other? Disregard slant range error.
Pressure altitude is 9500 feet. OAT is -25°C. If calibrated altitude is 10,000 feet, what is the true altitude?

There you are flying along at cruise altitude. Your First Officer decides to ask you, "Say, what amount of temperature rise is the temp probe experiencing?" You know the TAS is 315 knots and the probe's recovery coefficient is 1.0. Your trusty manual flight computer is hanging around your neck. You quickly give it a spin and answer:

choices.text: I need more information to answer that!

questions.question: You are in the maintenance hanger and want to help the mechanics compute an aircraft's CG. They put the aircraft on 3 scales and note the following: [[NEXT_LINE]] Nose Gear: 170 lbs at station 15" [[NEXT_LINE]] Left Main: 650 kg at station 90" [[NEXT_LINE]] Right Main: 600 kg at station 90" [[NEXT_LINE]] What is CG?
What is the square root of 538? (Provide an answer to nearest 1/10)

Tom, a former Navy pilot, one day recalls his training at Cecil Field. He remembers using an airfield to the northwest that was 5300 feet long with a 750 foot section marked off for practice carrier ops. He then wonders, what percentage of the field was not used?
If Tiffany and Tim are up front (40 inches), John and Genie are in back (80 inches), the fuel is onboard (70 inches), and the baggage is the back (100 inches), what is the total weight and CG?
questions.context: Tim has an aircraft with a basic empty weight of 1900 pounds (CG of 70 inches). Tim (180 pounds) has three friends he wants to take flying with him: Tiffany (120 pounds), John (270 pounds), and Genie (130 pounds). The fuel on board is 40 U.S. gallons of AVGAS and the baggage weighs 160 pounds.

questions.question: Using the above information, if it costs (non-fuel costs) $80 per minute to operate the G IV and $40 per minute to operate the King Air, which is cheaper to operate on their respective routes?
questions.context: You are flying for a corporation that has many corporate aircraft. On two separate days in two separate aircraft (a G IV and King Air), you are asked to fly from Madison, WI (800 feet MSL) to Rapid City, SD (2200 feet MSL). You fly in a standard atmosphere unless otherwise stated. Route 1 is with the G IV and route 2 is with the King Air and are as follows: Route 1 MSN to RAP direct (602 nautical miles) [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course = 287° G IV burns 2000 pounds of Jet A per hour and travels at Mach 0.77 (-45°C) Route 2 Leg 1 (MSN to DSM), Leg 2 (DSM to RAP) 294 nautical miles and 422 nautical miles respectively [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course = Leg 1 (248°) and Leg 2 (322°) King Air burns 750 pounds of Jet A per hour and travels at 230 knots, true airspeed Des Moines, IA (600 feet MSL) is a landing airport.

questions.question: You plan the flight for the King Air on Route 2 as follows: The King Air climbs at 900 feet per minute to FL240 with a true airspeed of 180 knots and descends at 1500 feet per minute from FL240 with a true airspeed of 260 knots. The winds aloft are calm. What is the distance in cruise for the entirety of Route 2?

On two separate days in two separate aircraft (a G IV and King Air), you are asked to fly from Madison, WI (800 feet MSL) to Rapid City, SD (2200 feet MSL). You fly in a standard atmosphere unless otherwise stated. questions.question: You plan the flight for the G IV on Route 1 as follows: The G IV climbs at 2000 feet per minute to FL380 with a true airspeed of 270 knots and descends at 2000 feet per minute from FL380 with an average true airspeed of 420 knots. The winds aloft are calm. What is the distance in cruise for Route 1?

questions.question: Julie crosses a ridge (elevation 6540 feet) at an indicated altitude of 11,500 feet. The temperature is +5°C and the altimeter setting is 29.22", what is her true altitude?

Maximum operating airspeed (Vmo) for the de Havilland DHC-8-201 at 14000' is 242 kts. Maximum operating airspeed (Vmo) for the de Havilland DHC-8-201 at 15000' is 239 kts. Maximum operating airspeed (Vmo) for the de Havilland DHC-8-201 at 20000' is 223 kts. What would you expect Vmo to be at 25000'?

Flying direct from Tokyo to San Fransisco at Flight Level 410, the DME shows you are at 88.5 nm from San Fransisco. Directly below is a ship that is 88.0 nm from San Fransisco, how many nautical miles above the earth's surface are you?
